preFix.title: Keeping ducks on a smallholding
preFix.subtitle: What ducks need that chickens do not, and the daily realities of a small duck flock.
preFix.excerpt: Ducks are hardy, productive, and good company, but they are not chickens. Their water requirements, messy habits, and nesting behaviour all differ. This reading covers breed choice, housing, water supply, and daily care for a small flock of laying or meat ducks kept alongside or separately from chickens.
preFix.sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Ducks (2002, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), chapter on ducks and geese, Internet Archive.
Dave Holderread, Storey's Guide to Raising Ducks (Storey Publishing, 2001), referenced for breed characterisation.

content.text: Ducks are among the most weather-resistant birds a smallholder can keep. A Khaki Campbell or Indian Runner will lay through a hard winter that has stopped chickens entirely. They are quiet, calm under handling, and largely parasite-resistant compared to chickens. What they ask for in return is a reliable water supply deep enough to submerge their bills, a predator-proof house, and a keeper who does not mind mud.
Choosing a breed

For eggs, Khaki Campbells are the standard choice: they lay 300 or more eggs per year, are small-bodied and economical on feed, and rarely go broody. Indian Runners are similar in temperament, upright in posture, and excellent foragers on a large run. For dual-purpose (eggs and table), the Aylesbury or Pekin provides a larger body and reasonable egg numbers. The Muscovy is the best choice for meat-only production: slow-growing but efficient on feed, and the females go broody reliably.
A flock of four to six ducks with one
drake

is a practical starting size. Keep no more than one drake per three to four ducks; a single duck with one drake will be harassed. Ducks without a drake lay just as well; the drake is only needed for fertile eggs.
Water

Ducks need water deep enough to submerge their bills and wash their nostrils. A standard chicken drinker is not enough. A trough or heavy-grade plastic basin 10 to 15 cm deep, refreshed daily, is the minimum. A small pond or paddling pool is better, though it needs emptying and refilling every few days or it becomes a stagnant hazard rather than a benefit.

Site the water away from the house and the feeding station. Ducks carry water in their bills to the feeding area and turn the ground around the feeder into deep mud within days. A concrete or slab base under the feeder, or moving the feeder regularly, keeps the surrounding ground workable.
Housing

Ducks do not perch. They nest and sleep at ground level, so a fox-proof house needs solid walls from floor to roof, not a raised coop with a pop-hole. A simple timber ark or a modified garden shed works well. Allow 0.5 square metres of floor space per bird. Ducks do not need nesting boxes; they lay their eggs in a chosen corner of the house, usually in the early morning, and a layer of straw in each corner gives them the option.
Ducks carry dense
down

insulation. They do not need supplementary heating unless the temperature falls below minus five degrees for extended periods. Ventilation matters more than warmth: a stuffy, damp house causes respiratory problems.
Feeding

Laying ducks need a waterfowl layers pellet rather than a chicken layers pellet. Chicken pellets can be used at a pinch but they are lower in niacin, and ducks on a niacin-deficient diet develop leg problems. Waterfowl pellets are available from most farm and feed suppliers. Supplement with mixed grain scattered in the run for foraging stimulation.
Eggs and brooding
Duck eggs have a thicker shell and a richer yolk than hen eggs; they are excellent for baking. Collect them from the house each morning. A Khaki Campbell or Indian Runner rarely forms a
clutch

and goes broody; a Muscovy female will sit reliably on a clutch of 10 to 14 eggs for 35 days. If you want to hatch Muscovy eggs, let the hen manage her own nest undisturbed in a quiet corner of the house.
Running ducks with chickens

Ducks and chickens can share a house and run, but the water supply quickly becomes a problem. Ducks wet the bedding around any water source; chickens develop respiratory disease in wet bedding. Separate water stations, or a separate duck run for the daytime, keeps the problem manageable. The flock hierarchy generally works itself out within a week of introduction.
